description: Build real fluency with grammar you can use, vocabulary that sticks, and listening and reading practice drawn from everyday life.

levels.title: Grammar
levels.description: The structural rules that hold sentences together, explained without the jargon.

lessons.title: Present Simple vs. Present Continuous
lessons.summary: Know when to say 'I work' and when to say 'I'm working'.
content: The present simple describes routines, facts, and things that are generally true: 'She works at a hospital.' The present continuous describes actions happening right now, or temporary situations: 'She's working a night shift this week.'

A common mistake is using the continuous form for permanent facts. We don't say 'I am living in Vietnam' to mean a permanent home — 'I live in Vietnam' is correct. Save the continuous form for things that are clearly temporary or in progress.

Stative verbs — like know, believe, want, and love — are rarely used in the continuous form at all, because they describe states, not actions. 'I am knowing the answer' is incorrect; 'I know the answer' is right.

vocabulary.translation: a sequence of actions regularly followed
lasting for only a limited time
a verb that describes a state rather than an action
done regularly, as a habit

lessons.title: The Present Perfect
lessons.summary: Connect the past to the present with 'have/has + past participle'.
content: The present perfect links a past action to the present moment. 'I have visited Berlin' tells us about a past experience without saying exactly when — the focus is on the fact that it happened, not the timing.

Use the present perfect with 'since' for a starting point ('since 2020') and 'for' for a duration ('for three years'). Compare this with the past simple, which needs a specific, finished time: 'I visited Berlin in 2019.'
Signal words like 'already', 'yet', 'just', and 'ever' almost always pair with the present perfect rather than the past simple in everyday conversation.

vocabulary.translation: knowledge or skill gained from doing something
the length of time something continues
before now, or sooner than expected
up until now (used in questions and negatives)

lessons.title: Conditionals: First and Second
lessons.summary: Talk about real possibilities and imagined situations.
content: The first conditional describes a realistic future result: 'If it rains, we will cancel the picnic.' The condition is likely or possible.
The second conditional imagines something unreal or unlikely: 'If I won the lottery, I would travel the world.' It uses the past tense in the if-clause, even though the meaning is present or future.
A useful test: if the situation could genuinely happen tomorrow, reach for the first conditional. If you're daydreaming or discussing something contrary to fact, use the second.

vocabulary.translation: a requirement that must be met for something to happen
imagined rather than real
probable; expected to happen
the opposite of what is true

levels.title: Vocabulary
levels.description: High-frequency words and phrases organized by everyday themes.

lessons.title: At the Office
lessons.summary: Words and phrases you'll hear in almost any workplace.
content: Workplace vocabulary tends to repeat across industries: meetings, deadlines, and colleagues appear whether you work in finance, design, or education.
Notice how many office phrases are built from phrasal verbs — 'follow up', 'circle back', 'touch base' — which don't translate word-for-word into other languages. Learn them as whole chunks.

vocabulary.translation: the latest time something must be finished
a person you work with
to check on progress after an initial action
to make brief contact to share an update
a list of topics to discuss in a meeting

lessons.title: Travel & Airports
lessons.summary: Navigate check-in, security, and boarding with confidence.
content: Airport vocabulary is highly predictable, which makes it an efficient set to memorize before a trip: you'll hear the same 30–40 words repeatedly across almost any airport in the world.
Pay attention to verbs that pair with specific nouns — you 'board' a flight, 'check in' luggage, and 'go through' security.

vocabulary.translation: the document that allows you onto a flight
a stop between flights on a journey
luggage small enough to bring into the cabin
the government checkpoint for goods entering a country
a period of time by which something is late

lessons.title: Describing Emotions
lessons.summary: Move beyond 'good' and 'bad' with precise emotional vocabulary.
content: Precise emotional vocabulary makes both speaking and writing far more expressive. Instead of 'I feel bad', you might say 'I feel overwhelmed' or 'I feel disheartened' — each carries a distinct shade of meaning.
Many emotion words come in intensity pairs: 'annoyed' is milder than 'furious'; 'content' is calmer than 'ecstatic'. Learning these pairs together helps you calibrate tone.

vocabulary.translation: having more than you can handle
having lost confidence or hope
in a state of peaceful satisfaction
extremely happy
anxious about something that may happen

levels.title: Listening
levels.description: Train your ear with natural pacing, contractions, and connected speech.

lessons.title: Understanding Connected Speech
lessons.summary: Why native speakers seem to 'skip' words — and how to catch them anyway.
content: In natural speech, words blend together. 'What are you doing' often sounds like 'whatcha doin'. This isn't sloppy speech — it's a predictable pattern called connected speech, and every fluent speaker does it.

Three patterns to listen for: linking (a consonant joins the next vowel, as in 'turn it off' → 'turn-it-off'), elision (a sound disappears, as in 'next day' losing its 't'), and assimilation (one sound changes to match its neighbor, as in 'handbag' sounding like 'hambag').
Practice by shadowing: listen to a short clip, pause, and repeat it aloud immediately, matching rhythm before worrying about individual words.

vocabulary.translation: the way words link and change in natural spoken language
the omission of a sound when speaking
a technique of repeating speech immediately after hearing it
a sound changing to become more like a nearby sound

lessons.title: Following a Podcast Conversation
lessons.summary: Strategies for keeping up with two speakers talking naturally.
content: Conversational podcasts move fast because speakers interrupt, agree, and build on each other's points. Don't aim to understand every word on a first listen — aim to track who agrees with whom and why.
Listen once for gist, a second time for specific details, and a third time with a transcript if you have one. This layered approach builds both top-down and bottom-up listening skills.

vocabulary.translation: the general meaning of something, without the details
a sudden change of subject away from the main topic
a written version of spoken words
playful, teasing conversation

levels.title: Reading
levels.description: Build reading stamina and speed with graded, real-world texts.

lessons.title: Skimming and Scanning
lessons.summary: Two fast-reading techniques that save you from reading word by word.
content: Skimming means reading quickly to get the general idea — titles, headings, first sentences of paragraphs. It's how experienced readers decide whether an article is worth reading closely.
Scanning means searching for a specific piece of information, like a date or a name, while ignoring everything else. Your eyes move in a pattern, not left-to-right line by line.
Combining both: skim an article first to build a mental map, then scan back through it to locate the exact detail you need.

vocabulary.translation: to read quickly for a general impression
to look through text quickly for specific information
an internal sense of how information is organized

lessons.title: Reading News Headlines
lessons.summary: Decode the compressed grammar unique to headlines.
content: Headlines drop small words to save space: articles ('a', 'the') and forms of 'to be' often disappear. 'Prices Rise Again' really means 'Prices Are Rising Again'.
Headlines also frequently use the present tense even for past events, to create a sense of immediacy — 'Storm Hits Coast' describes something that already happened.

vocabulary.translation: the title of a news article
the quality of feeling urgent or present
made shorter or more condensed

levels.title: Idioms
levels.description: Common expressions whose meaning isn't obvious from the individual words.

lessons.title: Idioms About Time
lessons.summary: 'Once in a blue moon' and other time-related expressions.
content: 'Once in a blue moon' means something happens very rarely. It has nothing to do with the moon's actual color — it refers to a rare second full moon in a calendar month.
'Beat the clock' means to finish something before a deadline, and 'in the nick of time' means at the last possible moment, just before it's too late.

vocabulary.translation: very rarely
to finish before a deadline
at the last possible moment
continuously, all day and night

lessons.title: Idioms About Effort
lessons.summary: Ways to describe hard work and giving your best.
content: 'Burn the midnight oil' means to work late into the night, from the days when oil lamps were needed to see after dark.
'Go the extra mile' means to make an additional effort beyond what's expected, and 'put your back into it' means to work with real physical or mental effort.

vocabulary.translation: to work late into the night
to make additional effort
to work hard at something
